title: My father's quiet Sundays
summary: Every Sunday at noon, the writer's father calls home — and for the first ten minutes, he says almost nothing. It took the writer twenty years to understand why.
body:
My father is a quiet man. He works in another city. He calls home every Sunday at noon. The call lasts thirty minutes. But for the first ten minutes, he does not say much.

When I was younger, I did not understand. I thought he did not want to talk. Now I am twenty.

His own father did not talk to him. They sat together in the evenings, in silence. My father learned, very young, that silence was a kind of attention.

When my father calls now, he listens. I tell him about school. I tell him what the neighbour's dog did. He says, 'Yes,' or 'I see.' After ten minutes, he starts to talk. He asks one question at a time. He waits for the answer.

Last year, my mother said, 'Your father's call is the loudest hour of his week.'

I did not understand. She explained. My father works alone. He fixes machines. From Monday to Saturday, he does not speak much. The thirty minutes on Sunday is, for him, talking. The first ten minutes are him remembering how.

I called him last Sunday. I let him be quiet for the first ten minutes. When he spoke, his voice was less heavy.

I will call him next Sunday.

annotations.meaningVi: nặng (ở đây theo nghĩa cảm xúc: nặng nề)
kéo dài (dùng cho khoảng thời gian)
một dạng, một loại — dùng khi so sánh hoặc mô tả điều gì đó không hoàn toàn nhưng gần giống
mỗi lần một, từng cái một (ví dụ: one question at a time = hỏi từng câu một)
bắt đầu nói chuyện — cấu trúc 'start to + động từ nguyên thể'
ồn ào nhất, to nhất — dạng so sánh nhất (superlative) của 'loud'
từ thứ Hai đến thứ Bảy — cụm thời gian chỉ khoảng thời gian trong tuần
để ông ấy yên lặng — cấu trúc 'let + tân ngữ + động từ nguyên thể' (không có 'to')

grammarNotes.title: Comparing past and present + 'less + adjective'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Để so sánh quá khứ với hiện tại: 'When I was younger, I did not understand. Now I am twenty.' Để chỉ một thay đổi nhỏ về mức độ, dùng 'less + tính từ': 'His voice was less heavy.' (Giọng ông nhẹ hơn — bớt nặng nề hơn.) Cấu trúc 'less' giảm mức tính từ; trái với 'more'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: To compare past and present: 'When I was younger, I did not understand. Now I am twenty.' For a small change in degree, use 'less + adjective': 'His voice was less heavy.' This reduces the adjective; the opposite is 'more'.

cloze.template:
My father is a {{b1}} man. He works in another city. He calls home every Sunday at {{b2}}. The call lasts {{b3}} minutes. But for the first {{b4}} minutes, he does not say much.

When I was younger, I did not understand. I thought he did not want to talk. Now I am {{b5}}.

His own father did not talk to him. They sat together in the evenings, in {{b6}}. My father learned, very young, that silence was a kind of attention.

When my father calls now, he listens. I tell him about {{b7}}. I tell him what the neighbour's dog did. He says, 'Yes,' or 'I see.' After ten minutes, he starts to talk. He asks one question at a time. He waits for the answer.

Last year, my mother said, 'Your father's call is the {{b8}} hour of his week.'

I did not understand. She explained. My father works alone. He fixes {{b9}}. From Monday to Saturday, he does not {{b10}} much. The thirty minutes on Sunday is, for him, talking. The first ten minutes are him remembering how.

I called him last Sunday. I let him be quiet for the first ten minutes. When he spoke, his voice was less {{b11}}.

I will call him {{b12}} Sunday.

blanks.explanation: For the first ten minutes, he does not say much.
They sat together in silence — and silence taught the writer's father about attention.
'Your father's call is the loudest hour of his week.' — said by the mother, surprisingly.
From Monday to Saturday, he does not speak much.
His voice was less heavy — less burdened. The first quiet ten minutes had helped.

translationVi:
Bố tôi là một người ít nói. Bố làm việc ở một thành phố khác. Mỗi Chủ Nhật trưa, bố gọi điện về nhà. Cuộc gọi kéo dài ba mươi phút. Nhưng trong mười phút đầu, bố không nói nhiều.

Khi tôi còn nhỏ, tôi không hiểu. Tôi tưởng bố không muốn nói chuyện. Giờ tôi hai mươi tuổi.

Ông nội tôi cũng không nói chuyện với bố. Hai người ngồi cạnh nhau buổi tối, trong yên lặng. Bố tôi học được, từ rất sớm, rằng sự im lặng cũng là một dạng chú ý.

Giờ khi bố gọi, bố lắng nghe. Tôi kể với bố về trường. Tôi kể bố nghe con chó nhà hàng xóm hôm nay làm gì. Bố nói 'Ừ' hoặc 'Bố biết rồi'. Sau mười phút, bố bắt đầu nói. Bố hỏi từng câu một. Bố chờ câu trả lời.

Năm ngoái, mẹ tôi nói: 'Cuộc gọi của bố con là giờ ồn nhất trong tuần của bố.'

Tôi không hiểu. Mẹ giải thích. Bố làm việc một mình. Bố sửa máy móc. Từ thứ Hai đến thứ Bảy, bố không nói chuyện nhiều. Ba mươi phút Chủ Nhật, với bố, là 'nói'. Mười phút đầu là bố đang nhớ lại cách nói.

Chủ Nhật vừa rồi tôi gọi bố. Tôi để bố yên lặng trong mười phút đầu. Khi bố nói, giọng bố nhẹ hơn một chút.

Chủ Nhật tới tôi sẽ gọi nữa.

questions.prompt: What time and day does the writer's father call?
questions.explanation: 'He calls home every Sunday at noon.'

questions.prompt: How long does the call last, and how long is he mostly silent?
options: Ten minutes; silent the whole time
Thirty minutes; silent for the first ten
questions.explanation: 'The call lasts thirty minutes. But for the first ten minutes, he does not say much.'

questions.prompt: What did the writer think when she was younger?
options: That her father had a bad phone
That her father did not want to talk
That her father did not love her
questions.explanation: 'I thought he did not want to talk.'

questions.prompt: What did the writer's father learn from his own father?
options: That silence was a kind of attention
questions.explanation: 'My father learned, very young, that silence was a kind of attention.'

questions.prompt: What does the father do during the call now?
options: Listens for ten minutes, then starts to ask one question at a time and wait for the answer
questions.explanation: 'When my father calls now, he listens.' / 'After ten minutes, he starts to talk. He asks one question at a time. He waits for the answer.'

questions.prompt: What did the writer's mother say about the Sunday call?
options: It is the loudest hour of his week
questions.explanation: 'Your father's call is the loudest hour of his week.'

questions.prompt: Why is the Sunday call 'the loudest hour' for him?
options: From Monday to Saturday he hardly speaks; Sunday is, for him, talking — and the first ten minutes are him remembering how
questions.explanation: 'From Monday to Saturday, he does not speak much.' / 'The first ten minutes are him remembering how.'

questions.prompt: What did the writer do differently last Sunday?
options: She let him be quiet for the first ten minutes
questions.explanation: 'I let him be quiet for the first ten minutes.'

questions.prompt: What did the writer notice when he spoke after the ten minutes?
questions.explanation: 'When he spoke, his voice was less heavy.'

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the story?
options: Silence can be a kind of attention; what looks like 'not talking' can be the start of real talking
questions.explanation: The whole story turns on the writer's late understanding of why the first ten minutes are quiet — and what happens after.
questions.hint: Look at how the writer reframes her father across the story.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer's mother calls the phone call her father's 'loudest hour of the week'. Think of someone in your life whose quiet — at first — seemed like 'not wanting to talk'. What might be happening in the quiet that you have not seen?
